BUNDLE-ADD(1)                                                    BUNDLE-ADD(1)

NNAAMMEE
       bbuunnddllee--aadddd - Add gem to the Gemfile and run bundle install

SSYYNNOOPPSSIISS
       bbuunnddllee aadddd _G_E_M___N_A_M_E [--group=GROUP] [--version=VERSION]
       [--source=SOURCE] [--skip-install] [--strict] [--optimistic]

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       Adds the named gem to the Gemfile and run bbuunnddllee iinnssttaallll. bbuunnddllee
       iinnssttaallll can be avoided by using the flag ----sskkiipp--iinnssttaallll.

       Example:

       bundle add rails

       bundle add rails --version "< 3.0, > 1.1"

       bundle add rails --version "~> 5.0.0" --source
       "https://gems.example.com" --group "development"

       bundle add rails --skip-install

       bundle add rails --group "development, test"

OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       ----vveerrssiioonn, --vv
              Specify version requirements(s) for the added gem.

       ----ggrroouupp, --gg
              Specify the group(s) for the added gem. Multiple groups should
              be separated by commas.

       ----ssoouurrccee, , --ss
              Specify the source for the added gem.

       ----sskkiipp--iinnssttaallll
              Adds the gem to the Gemfile but does not install it.

       ----ooppttiimmiissttiicc
              Adds optimistic declaration of version

       ----ssttrriicctt
              Adds strict declaration of version


                                 November 2018                   BUNDLE-ADD(1)
BUNDLE-BINSTUBS(1)                                          BUNDLE-BINSTUBS(1)

NNAAMMEE
       bbuunnddllee--bbiinnssttuubbss - Install the binstubs of the listed gems

SSYYNNOOPPSSIISS
       bbuunnddllee bbiinnssttuubbss _G_E_M___N_A_M_E [--force] [--path PATH] [--standalone]

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       Binstubs are scripts that wrap around executables. Bundler creates a
       small Ruby file (a binstub) that loads Bundler, runs the command, and
       puts it into bbiinn//. Binstubs are a shortcut-or alternative- to always
       using bbuunnddllee eexxeecc. This gives you a file that can by run directly, and
       one that will always run the correct gem version used by the
       application.

       For example, if you run bbuunnddllee bbiinnssttuubbss rrssppeecc--ccoorree, Bundler will create
       the file bbiinn//rrssppeecc. That file will contain enough code to load Bundler,
       tell it to load the bundled gems, and then run rspec.

       This command generates binstubs for executables in GGEEMM__NNAAMMEE. Binstubs
       are put into bbiinn, or the ----ppaatthh directory if one has been set. Calling
       binstubs with [GEM [GEM]] will create binstubs for all given gems.

OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       ----ffoorrccee
              Overwrite existing binstubs if they exist.

       ----ppaatthh The location to install the specified binstubs to. This defaults
              to bbiinn.

       ----ssttaannddaalloonnee
              Makes binstubs that can work without depending on Rubygems or
              Bundler at runtime.

       ----sshheebbaanngg
              Specify a different shebang executable name than the default
              (default ´ruby´)

BBUUNNDDLLEE IINNSSTTAALLLL ----BBIINNSSTTUUBBSS
       To create binstubs for all the gems in the bundle you can use the
       ----bbiinnssttuubbss flag in bundle install(1) _b_u_n_d_l_e_-_i_n_s_t_a_l_l_._1_._h_t_m_l.

                                 November 2018              BUNDLE-BINSTUBS(1)
BUNDLE-CHECK(1)                                                BUNDLE-CHECK(1)

NNAAMMEE
       bbuunnddllee--cchheecckk - Verifies if dependencies are satisfied by installed gems

SSYYNNOOPPSSIISS
       bbuunnddllee cchheecckk [--dry-run] [--gemfile=FILE] [--path=PATH]

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       cchheecckk searches the local machine for each of the gems requested in the
       Gemfile. If all gems are found, Bundler prints a success message and
       exits with a status of 0.

       If not, the first missing gem is listed and Bundler exits status 1.

OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       ----ddrryy--rruunn
              Locks the [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)] before running the command.

       ----ggeemmffiillee
              Use the specified gemfile instead of the
              [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)].

       ----ppaatthh Specify a different path than the system default ($$BBUUNNDDLLEE__PPAATTHH
              or $$GGEEMM__HHOOMMEE). Bundler will remember this value for future
              installs on this machine.


                                 November 2018                 BUNDLE-CHECK(1)
BUNDLE-CLEAN(1)                                                BUNDLE-CLEAN(1)

NNAAMMEE
       bbuunnddllee--cclleeaann - Cleans up unused gems in your bundler directory

SSYYNNOOPPSSIISS
       bbuunnddllee cclleeaann [--dry-run] [--force]

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       This command will remove all unused gems in your bundler directory.
       This is useful when you have made many changes to your gem
       dependencies.

OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       ----ddrryy--rruunn
              Print the changes, but do not clean the unused gems.

       ----ffoorrccee
              Force a clean even if ----ppaatthh is not set.


                                 November 2018                 BUNDLE-CLEAN(1)
BUNDLE-CONFIG(1)                                              BUNDLE-CONFIG(1)

NNAAMMEE
       bbuunnddllee--ccoonnffiigg - Set bundler configuration options

SSYYNNOOPPSSIISS
       bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg [_n_a_m_e [_v_a_l_u_e]]

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       This command allows you to interact with Bundler´s configuration
       system.

       Bundler loads configuration settings in this order:

       1.  Local config (aapppp//..bbuunnddllee//ccoonnffiigg)

       2.  Environmental variables (EENNVV)

       3.  Global config (~~//..bbuunnddllee//ccoonnffiigg)

       4.  Bundler default config




       Executing bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg with no parameters will print a list of all
       bundler configuration for the current bundle, and where that
       configuration was set.

       Executing bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg <<nnaammee>> will print the value of that
       configuration setting, and where it was set.

       Executing bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg <<nnaammee>> <<vvaalluuee>> will set that configuration to
       the value specified for all bundles executed as the current user. The
       configuration will be stored in ~~//..bbuunnddllee//ccoonnffiigg. If _n_a_m_e already is
       set, _n_a_m_e will be overridden and user will be warned.

       Executing bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg ----gglloobbaall <<nnaammee>> <<vvaalluuee>> works the same as
       above.

       Executing bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg ----llooccaall <<nnaammee>> <<vvaalluuee>> will set that
       configuration to the local application. The configuration will be
       stored in aapppp//..bbuunnddllee//ccoonnffiigg.

       Executing bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg ----ddeelleettee <<nnaammee>> will delete the configuration
       in both local and global sources. Not compatible with --global or
       --local flag.

       Executing bundle with the BBUUNNDDLLEE__IIGGNNOORREE__CCOONNFFIIGG environment variable set
       will cause it to ignore all configuration.

       Executing bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg ddiissaabbllee__mmuullttiissoouurrccee ttrruuee upgrades the warning
       about the Gemfile containing multiple primary sources to an error.
       Executing bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg ----ddeelleettee ddiissaabbllee__mmuullttiissoouurrccee downgrades this
       error to a warning.

RREEMMEEMMBBEERRIINNGG OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       Flags passed to bbuunnddllee iinnssttaallll or the Bundler runtime, such as ----ppaatthh
       ffoooo or ----wwiitthhoouutt pprroodduuccttiioonn, are not remembered between commands. If
       these options must be remembered,they must be set using bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg
       (e.g., bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg ppaatthh ffoooo).

       The options that can be configured are:

       bbiinn    Creates a directory (defaults to ~~//bbiinn) and place any
              executables from the gem there. These executables run in
              Bundler´s context. If used, you might add this directory to your
              environment´s PPAATTHH variable. For instance, if the rraaiillss gem
              comes with a rraaiillss executable, this flag will create a bbiinn//rraaiillss
              executable that ensures that all referred dependencies will be
              resolved using the bundled gems.

       ddeeppllooyymmeenntt
              In deployment mode, Bundler will ´roll-out´ the bundle for
              pprroodduuccttiioonn use. Please check carefully if you want to have this
              option enabled in ddeevveellooppmmeenntt or tteesstt environments.

       ppaatthh   The location to install the specified gems to. This defaults to
              Rubygems´ setting. Bundler shares this location with Rubygems,
              ggeemm iinnssttaallll ...... will have gem installed there, too. Therefore,
              gems installed without a ----ppaatthh ...... setting will show up by
              calling ggeemm lliisstt. Accordingly, gems installed to other locations
              will not get listed.

       wwiitthhoouutt
              A space-separated list of groups referencing gems to skip during
              installation.

       wwiitthh   A space-separated list of groups referencing gems to include
              during installation.

BBUUIILLDD OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       You can use bbuunnddllee ccoonnffiigg to give Bundler the flags to pass to the gem
       installer every time bundler tries to install a particular gem.

       A very common example, the mmyyssqqll gem, requires Snow Leopard users to
       pass configuration flags to ggeemm iinnssttaallll to specify where to find the
       mmyyssqqll__ccoonnffiigg executable.



           gem install mysql -- --with-mysql-config=/usr/local/mysql/bin/mysql_config




       Since the specific location of that executable can change from machine
       to machine, you can specify these flags on a per-machine basis.



           bundle config build.mysql --with-mysql-config=/usr/local/mysql/bin/mysql_config




       After running this command, every time bundler needs to install the
       mmyyssqqll gem, it will pass along the flags you specified.

CCOONNFFIIGGUURRAATTIIOONN KKEEYYSS
       Configuration keys in bundler have two forms: the canonical form and
       the environment variable form.

       For instance, passing the ----wwiitthhoouutt flag to bundle install(1)
       _b_u_n_d_l_e_-_i_n_s_t_a_l_l_._1_._h_t_m_l prevents Bundler from installing certain groups
       specified in the Gemfile(5). Bundler persists this value in
       aapppp//..bbuunnddllee//ccoonnffiigg so that calls to BBuunnddlleerr..sseettuupp do not try to find
       gems from the GGeemmffiillee that you didn´t install. Additionally, subsequent
       calls to bundle install(1) _b_u_n_d_l_e_-_i_n_s_t_a_l_l_._1_._h_t_m_l remember this setting
       and skip those groups.

       The canonical form of this configuration is ""wwiitthhoouutt"". To convert the
       canonical form to the environment variable form, capitalize it, and
       prepend BBUUNNDDLLEE__. The environment variable form of ""wwiitthhoouutt"" is
       BBUUNNDDLLEE__WWIITTHHOOUUTT.

       Any periods in the configuration keys must be replaced with two
       underscores when setting it via environment variables. The
       configuration key llooccaall..rraacckk becomes the environment variable
       BBUUNNDDLLEE__LLOOCCAALL____RRAACCKK.

LLIISSTT OOFF AAVVAAIILLAABBLLEE KKEEYYSS
       The following is a list of all configuration keys and their purpose.
       You can learn more about their operation in bundle install(1)
       _b_u_n_d_l_e_-_i_n_s_t_a_l_l_._1_._h_t_m_l.

       •   aallllooww__bbuunnddlleerr__ddeeppeennddeennccyy__ccoonnfflliiccttss
           (BBUUNNDDLLEE__AALLLLOOWW__BBUUNNDDLLEERR__DDEEPPEENNDDEENNCCYY__CCOONNFFLLIICCTTSS): Allow resolving to
           specifications that have dependencies on bbuunnddlleerr that are
           incompatible with the running Bundler version.

       •   aallllooww__ddeeppllooyymmeenntt__ssoouurrccee__ccrreeddeennttiiaall__cchhaannggeess
           (BBUUNNDDLLEE__AALLLLOOWW__DDEEPPLLOOYYMMEENNTT__SSOOUURRCCEE__CCRREEDDEENNTTIIAALL__CCHHAANNGGEESS): When in
           deployment mode, allow changing the credentials to a gem´s source.
           Ex: hhttttppss::////ssoommee..hhoosstt..ccoomm//ggeemmss//ppaatthh// ->
           hhttttppss::////uusseerr__nnaammee::ppaasssswwoorrdd@@ssoommee..hhoosstt..ccoomm//ggeemmss//ppaatthh

       •   aallllooww__oofffflliinnee__iinnssttaallll (BBUUNNDDLLEE__AALLLLOOWW__OOFFFFLLIINNEE__IINNSSTTAALLLL): Allow Bundler
           to use cached data when installing without network access.

       •   aauuttoo__cclleeaann__wwiitthhoouutt__ppaatthh (BBUUNNDDLLEE__AAUUTTOO__CCLLEEAANN__WWIITTHHOOUUTT__PPAATTHH):
           Automatically run bbuunnddllee cclleeaann after installing when an explicit
           ppaatthh has not been set and Bundler is not installing into the system
           gems.

       •   aauuttoo__iinnssttaallll (BBUUNNDDLLEE__AAUUTTOO__IINNSSTTAALLLL): Automatically run bbuunnddllee
           iinnssttaallll when gems are missing.

       •   bbiinn (BBUUNNDDLLEE__BBIINN): Install executables from gems in the bundle to
           the specified directory. Defaults to ffaallssee.

       •   ccaacchhee__aallll (BBUUNNDDLLEE__CCAACCHHEE__AALLLL): Cache all gems, including path and
           git gems.

       •   ccaacchhee__aallll__ppllaattffoorrmmss (BBUUNNDDLLEE__CCAACCHHEE__AALLLL__PPLLAATTFFOORRMMSS): Cache gems for
           all platforms.

       •   ccaacchhee__ppaatthh (BBUUNNDDLLEE__CCAACCHHEE__PPAATTHH): The directory that bundler will
           place cached gems in when running bbuunnddllee ppaacckkaaggee, and that bundler
           will look in when installing gems. Defaults to vveennddoorr//bbuunnddllee.

       •   cclleeaann (BBUUNNDDLLEE__CCLLEEAANN): Whether Bundler should run bbuunnddllee cclleeaann
           automatically after bbuunnddllee iinnssttaallll.

       •   ccoonnssoollee (BBUUNNDDLLEE__CCOONNSSOOLLEE): The console that bbuunnddllee ccoonnssoollee starts.
           Defaults to iirrbb.

       •   ddeeffaauulltt__iinnssttaallll__uusseess__ppaatthh (BBUUNNDDLLEE__DDEEFFAAUULLTT__IINNSSTTAALLLL__UUSSEESS__PPAATTHH):
           Whether a bbuunnddllee iinnssttaallll without an explicit ----ppaatthh argument
           defaults to installing gems in ..bbuunnddllee.

       •   ddeeppllooyymmeenntt (BBUUNNDDLLEE__DDEEPPLLOOYYMMEENNTT): Disallow changes to the GGeemmffiillee.
           When the GGeemmffiillee is changed and the lockfile has not been updated,
           running Bundler commands will be blocked.

       •   ddiissaabbllee__cchheecckkssuumm__vvaalliiddaattiioonn (BBUUNNDDLLEE__DDIISSAABBLLEE__CCHHEECCKKSSUUMM__VVAALLIIDDAATTIIOONN):
           Allow installing gems even if they do not match the checksum
           provided by RubyGems.

       •   ddiissaabbllee__eexxeecc__llooaadd (BBUUNNDDLLEE__DDIISSAABBLLEE__EEXXEECC__LLOOAADD): Stop Bundler from
           using llooaadd to launch an executable in-process in bbuunnddllee eexxeecc.

       •   ddiissaabbllee__llooccaall__bbrraanncchh__cchheecckk (BBUUNNDDLLEE__DDIISSAABBLLEE__LLOOCCAALL__BBRRAANNCCHH__CCHHEECCKK):
           Allow Bundler to use a local git override without a branch
           specified in the Gemfile.

       •   ddiissaabbllee__mmuullttiissoouurrccee (BBUUNNDDLLEE__DDIISSAABBLLEE__MMUULLTTIISSOOUURRCCEE): When set,
           Gemfiles containing multiple sources will produce errors instead of

       You can set them globally either via environment variables or bbuunnddllee
       ccoonnffiigg, whichever is preferable for your setup. If you use both,
       environment variables will take preference over global settings.

LLOOCCAALL GGIITT RREEPPOOSS
       Bundler also allows you to work against a git repository locally
       instead of using the remote version. This can be achieved by setting up
       a local override:



           bundle config local.GEM_NAME /path/to/local/git/repository




       For example, in order to use a local Rack repository, a developer could
       call:



           bundle config local.rack ~/Work/git/rack




       Now instead of checking out the remote git repository, the local
       override will be used. Similar to a path source, every time the local
       git repository change, changes will be automatically picked up by
       Bundler. This means a commit in the local git repo will update the
       revision in the GGeemmffiillee..lloocckk to the local git repo revision. This
       requires the same attention as git submodules. Before pushing to the
       remote, you need to ensure the local override was pushed, otherwise you
       may point to a commit that only exists in your local machine. You´ll
       also need to CGI escape your usernames and passwords as well.

       Bundler does many checks to ensure a developer won´t work with invalid
       references. Particularly, we force a developer to specify a branch in
       the GGeemmffiillee in order to use this feature. If the branch specified in
       the GGeemmffiillee and the current branch in the local git repository do not
       match, Bundler will abort. This ensures that a developer is always
       working against the correct branches, and prevents accidental locking
       to a different branch.

       Finally, Bundler also ensures that the current revision in the
       GGeemmffiillee..lloocckk exists in the local git repository. By doing this, Bundler
       forces you to fetch the latest changes in the remotes.

MMIIRRRROORRSS OOFF GGEEMM SSOOUURRCCEESS
       Bundler supports overriding gem sources with mirrors. This allows you
       to configure rubygems.org as the gem source in your Gemfile while still
       using your mirror to fetch gems.



           bundle config mirror.SOURCE_URL MIRROR_URL




       For example, to use a mirror of rubygems.org hosted at
       rubygems-mirror.org:



           bundle config mirror.http://rubygems.org http://rubygems-mirror.org




       Each mirror also provides a fallback timeout setting. If the mirror
       does not respond within the fallback timeout, Bundler will try to use
       the original server instead of the mirror.



           bundle config mirror.SOURCE_URL.fallback_timeout TIMEOUT




       For example, to fall back to rubygems.org after 3 seconds:



           bundle config mirror.https://rubygems.org.fallback_timeout 3




       The default fallback timeout is 0.1 seconds, but the setting can
       currently only accept whole seconds (for example, 1, 15, or 30).

CCRREEDDEENNTTIIAALLSS FFOORR GGEEMM SSOOUURRCCEESS
       Bundler allows you to configure credentials for any gem source, which
       allows you to avoid putting secrets into your Gemfile.



           bundle config SOURCE_HOSTNAME USERNAME:PASSWORD




       For example, to save the credentials of user ccllaauuddeettttee for the gem
       source at ggeemmss..lloonnggeerroouuss..ccoomm, you would run:



           bundle config gems.longerous.com claudette:s00pers3krit




       Or you can set the credentials as an environment variable like this:



           export BUNDLE_GEMS__LONGEROUS__COM="claudette:s00pers3krit"




       For gems with a git source with HTTP(S) URL you can specify credentials
       like so:



           bundle config https://github.com/bundler/bundler.git username:password




       Or you can set the credentials as an environment variable like so:



           export BUNDLE_GITHUB__COM=username:password




       This is especially useful for private repositories on hosts such as
       Github, where you can use personal OAuth tokens:



           export BUNDLE_GITHUB__COM=abcd0123generatedtoken:x-oauth-basic




CCOONNFFIIGGUURREE BBUUNNDDLLEERR DDIIRREECCTTOORRIIEESS
       Bundler´s home, config, cache and plugin directories are able to be
       configured through environment variables. The default location for
       Bundler´s home directory is ~~//..bbuunnddllee, which all directories inherit
       from by default. The following outlines the available environment
       variables and their default values



           BUNDLE_USER_HOME : $HOME/.bundle
           BUNDLE_USER_CACHE : $BUNDLE_USER_HOME/cache
           BUNDLE_USER_CONFIG : $BUNDLE_USER_HOME/config
           BUNDLE_USER_PLUGIN : $BUNDLE_USER_HOME/plugin





                                 December 2018                BUNDLE-CONFIG(1)
BUNDLE-DOCTOR(1)                                              BUNDLE-DOCTOR(1)

NNAAMMEE
       bbuunnddllee--ddooccttoorr - Checks the bundle for common problems

SSYYNNOOPPSSIISS
       bbuunnddllee ddooccttoorr [--quiet] [--gemfile=GEMFILE]

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       Checks your Gemfile and gem environment for common problems. If issues
       are detected, Bundler prints them and exits status 1. Otherwise,
       Bundler prints a success message and exits status 0.

       Examples of common problems caught by bundle-doctor include:

       •   Invalid Bundler settings

       •   Mismatched Ruby versions

       •   Mismatched platforms

       •   Uninstalled gems

       •   Missing dependencies




OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       ----qquuiieett
              Only output warnings and errors.

       ----ggeemmffiillee==<<ggeemmffiillee>>
              The location of the Gemfile(5) which Bundler should use. This
              defaults to a Gemfile(5) in the current working directory. In
              general, Bundler will assume that the location of the Gemfile(5)
              is also the project´s root and will try to find GGeemmffiillee..lloocckk and
              vveennddoorr//ccaacchhee relative to this location.

NNAAMMEE
       bbuunnddllee--eexxeecc - Execute a command in the context of the bundle

SSYYNNOOPPSSIISS
       bbuunnddllee eexxeecc [--keep-file-descriptors] _c_o_m_m_a_n_d

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       This command executes the command, making all gems specified in the
       [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)] available to rreeqquuiirree in Ruby programs.

       Essentially, if you would normally have run something like rrssppeecc
       ssppeecc//mmyy__ssppeecc..rrbb, and you want to use the gems specified in the
       [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)] and installed via bundle install(1)
       _b_u_n_d_l_e_-_i_n_s_t_a_l_l_._1_._h_t_m_l, you should run bbuunnddllee eexxeecc rrssppeecc
       ssppeecc//mmyy__ssppeecc..rrbb.

       Note that bbuunnddllee eexxeecc does not require that an executable is available
       on your shell´s $$PPAATTHH.

OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       ----kkeeeepp--ffiillee--ddeessccrriippttoorrss
              Exec in Ruby 2.0 began discarding non-standard file descriptors.
              When this flag is passed, exec will revert to the 1.9 behaviour
              of passing all file descriptors to the new process.

BBUUNNDDLLEE IINNSSTTAALLLL ----BBIINNSSTTUUBBSS
       If you use the ----bbiinnssttuubbss flag in bundle install(1)
       _b_u_n_d_l_e_-_i_n_s_t_a_l_l_._1_._h_t_m_l, Bundler will automatically create a directory
       (which defaults to aapppp__rroooott//bbiinn) containing all of the executables
       available from gems in the bundle.

       After using ----bbiinnssttuubbss, bbiinn//rrssppeecc ssppeecc//mmyy__ssppeecc..rrbb is identical to
       bbuunnddllee eexxeecc rrssppeecc ssppeecc//mmyy__ssppeecc..rrbb.

EENNVVIIRROONNMMEENNTT MMOODDIIFFIICCAATTIIOONNSS
       bbuunnddllee eexxeecc makes a number of changes to the shell environment, then
       executes the command you specify in full.

       •   make sure that it´s still possible to shell out to bbuunnddllee from
           inside a command invoked by bbuunnddllee eexxeecc (using $$BBUUNNDDLLEE__BBIINN__PPAATTHH)

       •   put the directory containing executables (like rraaiillss, rrssppeecc,
           rraacckkuupp) for your bundle on $$PPAATTHH

       •   make sure that if bundler is invoked in the subshell, it uses the
           same GGeemmffiillee (by setting BBUUNNDDLLEE__GGEEMMFFIILLEE)

       •   add --rrbbuunnddlleerr//sseettuupp to $$RRUUBBYYOOPPTT, which makes sure that Ruby
           programs invoked in the subshell can see the gems in the bundle




       It also modifies Rubygems:

       •   disallow loading additional gems not in the bundle

       •   modify the ggeemm method to be a no-op if a gem matching the
           requirements is in the bundle, and to raise a GGeemm::::LLooaaddEErrrroorr if
           it´s not

       •   Define GGeemm..rreeffrreesshh to be a no-op, since the source index is always
           frozen when using bundler, and to prevent gems from the system
           leaking into the environment

       •   Override GGeemm..bbiinn__ppaatthh to use the gems in the bundle, making system
           executables work

       •   Add all gems in the bundle into Gem.loaded_specs




       Finally, bbuunnddllee eexxeecc also implicitly modifies GGeemmffiillee..lloocckk if the
       lockfile and the Gemfile do not match. Bundler needs the Gemfile to
       determine things such as a gem´s groups, aauuttoorreeqquuiirree, and platforms,
       etc., and that information isn´t stored in the lockfile. The Gemfile
       and lockfile must be synced in order to bbuunnddllee eexxeecc successfully, so
       bbuunnddllee eexxeecc updates the lockfile beforehand.

   LLooaaddiinngg
       By default, when attempting to bbuunnddllee eexxeecc to a file with a ruby
       shebang, Bundler will KKeerrnneell..llooaadd that file instead of using
       KKeerrnneell..eexxeecc. For the vast majority of cases, this is a performance
       improvement. In a rare few cases, this could cause some subtle
       side-effects (such as dependence on the exact contents of $$00 or
       ____FFIILLEE____) and the optimization can be disabled by enabling the
       ddiissaabbllee__eexxeecc__llooaadd setting.

   SShheelllliinngg oouutt
       Any Ruby code that opens a subshell (like ssyysstteemm, backticks, or %%xx{{}})
       will automatically use the current Bundler environment. If you need to
       shell out to a Ruby command that is not part of your current bundle,
       use the wwiitthh__cclleeaann__eennvv method with a block. Any subshells created
       inside the block will be given the environment present before Bundler
       was activated. For example, Homebrew commands run Ruby, but don´t work
       inside a bundle:



           Bundler.with_clean_env do
             `brew install wget`
           end




       Using wwiitthh__cclleeaann__eennvv is also necessary if you are shelling out to a
       different bundle. Any Bundler commands run in a subshell will inherit
       the current Gemfile, so commands that need to run in the context of a
       different bundle also need to use wwiitthh__cclleeaann__eennvv.



           Bundler.with_clean_env do
             Dir.chdir "/other/bundler/project" do
               `bundle exec ./script`
             end
           end




       Bundler provides convenience helpers that wrap ssyysstteemm and eexxeecc, and
       they can be used like this:



           Bundler.clean_system(´brew install wget´)
           Bundler.clean_exec(´brew install wget´)




RRUUBBYYGGEEMMSS PPLLUUGGIINNSS
       At present, the Rubygems plugin system requires all files named
       rruubbyyggeemmss__pplluuggiinn..rrbb on the load path of _a_n_y installed gem when any Ruby
       code requires rruubbyyggeemmss..rrbb. This includes executables installed into the
       system, like rraaiillss, rraacckkuupp, and rrssppeecc.

       Since Rubygems plugins can contain arbitrary Ruby code, they commonly
       end up activating themselves or their dependencies.

       For instance, the ggeemmccuutttteerr 00..55 gem depended on jjssoonn__ppuurree. If you had
       that version of gemcutter installed (even if you _a_l_s_o had a newer
       version without this problem), Rubygems would activate ggeemmccuutttteerr 00..55
       and jjssoonn__ppuurree <<llaatteesstt>>.

       If your Gemfile(5) also contained jjssoonn__ppuurree (or a gem with a dependency
       on jjssoonn__ppuurree), the latest version on your system might conflict with
       the version in your Gemfile(5), or the snapshot version in your
       GGeemmffiillee..lloocckk.

       If this happens, bundler will say:



           You have already activated json_pure 1.4.6 but your Gemfile
           requires json_pure 1.4.3. Consider using bundle exec.




       In this situation, you almost certainly want to remove the underlying
       gem with the problematic gem plugin. In general, the authors of these
       plugins (in this case, the ggeemmccuutttteerr gem) have released newer versions
       that are more careful in their plugins.

       You can find a list of all the gems containing gem plugins by running



           ruby -rubygems -e "puts Gem.find_files(´rubygems_plugin.rb´)"




       At the very least, you should remove all but the newest version of each
       gem plugin, and also remove all gem plugins that you aren´t using (ggeemm
       uunniinnssttaallll ggeemm__nnaammee).

       bbuunnddllee--ggeemm - Generate a project skeleton for creating a rubygem

SSYYNNOOPPSSIISS
       bbuunnddllee ggeemm _G_E_M___N_A_M_E _O_P_T_I_O_N_S

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       Generates a directory named GGEEMM__NNAAMMEE with a RRaakkeeffiillee, GGEEMM__NNAAMMEE..ggeemmssppeecc,
       and other supporting files and directories that can be used to develop
       a rubygem with that name.

       Run rraakkee --TT in the resulting project for a list of Rake tasks that can
       be used to test and publish the gem to rubygems.org.

       The generated project skeleton can be customized with OPTIONS, as
       explained below. Note that these options can also be specified via
       Bundler´s global configuration file using the following names:

       •   ggeemm..ccoocc

       •   ggeemm..mmiitt

       •   ggeemm..tteesstt




OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       ----eexxee or --bb or ----bbiinn
              Specify that Bundler should create a binary executable (as
              eexxee//GGEEMM__NNAAMMEE) in the generated rubygem project. This binary will
              also be added to the GGEEMM__NNAAMMEE..ggeemmssppeecc manifest. This behavior is
              disabled by default.

       ----nnoo--eexxee
              Do not create a binary (overrides ----eexxee specified in the global
              config).

       ----ccoocc  Add a CCOODDEE__OOFF__CCOONNDDUUCCTT..mmdd file to the root of the generated
              project. If this option is unspecified, an interactive prompt
              will be displayed and the answer will be saved in Bundler´s
              global config for future bbuunnddllee ggeemm use.

       ----nnoo--ccoocc
              Do not create a CCOODDEE__OOFF__CCOONNDDUUCCTT..mmdd (overrides ----ccoocc specified in
              the global config).

       ----eexxtt  Add boilerplate for C extension code to the generated project.
              This behavior is disabled by default.

       ----nnoo--eexxtt
              Do not add C extension code (overrides ----eexxtt specified in the
              global config).

       ----mmiitt  Add an MIT license to a LLIICCEENNSSEE..ttxxtt file in the root of the
              generated project. Your name from the global git config is used
              for the copyright statement. If this option is unspecified, an
              interactive prompt will be displayed and the answer will be
              saved in Bundler´s global config for future bbuunnddllee ggeemm use.

       ----nnoo--mmiitt
              Do not create a LLIICCEENNSSEE..ttxxtt (overrides ----mmiitt specified in the
              global config).

       --tt, ----tteesstt==mmiinniitteesstt, ----tteesstt==rrssppeecc
              Specify the test framework that Bundler should use when
              generating the project. Acceptable values are mmiinniitteesstt and
              rrssppeecc. The GGEEMM__NNAAMMEE..ggeemmssppeecc will be configured and a skeleton
              test/spec directory will be created based on this option. If
              this option is unspecified, an interactive prompt will be
              displayed and the answer will be saved in Bundler´s global
              config for future bbuunnddllee ggeemm use. If no option is specified, the
              default testing framework is RSpec.

       --ee, ----eeddiitt[[==EEDDIITTOORR]]
              Open the resulting GEM_NAME.gemspec in EDITOR, or the default
              editor if not specified. The default is $$BBUUNNDDLLEERR__EEDDIITTOORR,
              $$VVIISSUUAALL, or $$EEDDIITTOORR.

       •   bundle config(1) _b_u_n_d_l_e_-_c_o_n_f_i_g_._1_._h_t_m_l

       bbuunnddllee--iinniitt - Generates a Gemfile into the current working directory

       bbuunnddllee iinniitt [--gemspec=FILE]

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       Init generates a default [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)] in the current
       working directory. When adding a [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)] to a gem with
       a gemspec, the ----ggeemmssppeecc option will automatically add each dependency
       listed in the gemspec file to the newly created
       [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)].

OOPPTTIIOONNSS
       ----ggeemmssppeecc
              Use the specified .gemspec to create the
              [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)]

FFIILLEESS
       Included in the default [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)] generated is the line
       ## ffrroozzeenn__ssttrriinngg__lliitteerraall:: ttrruuee. This is a magic comment supported for

       string literals in the file being implicitly frozen.

NNAAMMEE
       bbuunnddllee--iinnjjeecctt - Add named gem(s) with version requirements to Gemfile

SSYYNNOOPPSSIISS
       bbuunnddllee iinnjjeecctt [GEM] [VERSION]

DDEESSCCRRIIPPTTIIOONN
       Adds the named gem(s) with their version requirements to the resolved
       [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)].

       This command will add the gem to both your [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)] and
       Gemfile.lock if it isn´t listed yet.

       Example:



           bundle install
           bundle inject ´rack´ ´> 0´




       This will inject the ´rack´ gem with a version greater than 0 in your
       [GGeemmffiillee((55))][Gemfile(5)] and Gemfile.lock
